turns.text: Hello Alycia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for asking. Honestly, it’s a bit strange to be on this side of the desk. Usually, I’m the one holding the clipboard, explaining the "why" behind every procedure, but now... now I’m just Alycia.

Coming home to the apartment in Middleborough Center—it’s just so quiet. It’s supposed to be peaceful, right? But the silence is deafening when you’re staring at her. I’m twenty-one, and my friends are out celebrating finishing their degrees, and here I am, counting her breaths at 3 a.m. because I’m terrified I’ll miss a sign of respiratory distress. I know the protocols by heart—I’ve charted them a thousand times—but it’s different when it’s your own daughter.

I think the hardest part is that I can’t turn the "nurse" part of my brain off. When the visiting nurse came, I caught myself using all the clinical shorthand just to keep up this facade of being the capable professional. I was scared that if I asked a "dumb" question, they’d look at me like I’d lost my mind. I don’t want to be the "nurse mom" who should already know everything. I just want to be allowed to be a mother who’s scared and tired, who doesn't have all the answers. It’s… it’s a very lonely kind of weight to carry.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think I was arrogant, honestly. Because I work in neonatal health, I spent so much time focused on the baby—the Apgar scores, the bilirubin levels, the feeding charts—that I think I subconsciously treated my own body like a piece of equipment that would just "reset" once the delivery was over. I expected a clean, clinical recovery. I thought, *I know the anatomy, I know the healing timelines, I’ll be back to myself in a few weeks.*

The reality? It was jarring. It’s one thing to explain the physiology of postpartum healing to a patient; it’s another thing to feel your own body betrayal—the fatigue that settles into your marrow, the sheer, physical fragility of it all. I felt like I was wearing a stranger's body. 

And because I’m a nurse, I think I expected myself to be an "ideal patient"—to be stoic and handle the pain without making a fuss. But being at home, trying to navigate those first weeks while feeling like I was falling apart, was a shock. I had this expectation that my medical knowledge would protect me from the messiness of it, but it actually just made me more critical of my own healing. I felt like I should be "performing" better than I was. It’s hard to admit that even with the training, I still felt completely lost.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I laugh a little when I think about my expectations. I’ve coached so many mothers through that first latch, adjusted their positions, and confidently told them, "It’s the most natural thing in the world." I treated breastfeeding like a clinical task—a series of steps to follow: stimulation, suck, swallow, repeat. I honestly thought that because I knew the mechanics of how to help a baby latch, I would breeze through it. I thought I’d be the one demonstrating for everyone else.

The reality was a complete reality check. When I sat there in my bedroom at two in the morning, exhausted and feeling like a failure because she wouldn't latch properly, I didn't feel like a nurse. I felt like a young, overwhelmed woman who couldn't feed her own child. It was physically painful, yes, but the emotional side was worse. I felt this intense, irrational shame—like I was a fraud for having all that "expertise" and yet still sitting there crying because I was frustrated and my nipples were raw. 

I didn't want a consultant; I wanted someone to hold my hand and tell me I wasn't doing it wrong. I realized that knowing the science of lactation doesn't prepare you for the raw, vulnerable intimacy—or the utter exhaustion—of actually doing it yourself.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I expected to be the "cool, calm, and collected" new mom. I told myself that because I’d seen it all in the NICU and the pediatric ward, I’d be immune to the "baby blues" or the postpartum fog. I genuinely thought my clinical background would act as a buffer. I expected to feel empowered, maybe a little tired, but mostly just confident in my ability to manage her health. I thought I would be the one writing the manual.

The reality is that I’m living in a state of hyper-vigilance that I can’t seem to switch off. It’s not just "new mom nerves"—it’s like I have a monitor running in my brain 24/7. When I see her sleep, I’m mentally charting her respiratory rate and scanning for retractions. It’s exhausting to feel like you’re "on duty" even when you’re in your own home.

I didn't expect the isolation. I’m surrounded by people at work who respect my brain, but here, I feel like a young woman who doesn't know who she is outside of this "medium risk" chart label. There’s a lot of fear underneath the surface—the fear of being judged by my peers if I admit I’m struggling, and the fear that I’m failing because I’m letting my medical mind override my heart. It’s a really heavy, lonely way to start motherhood.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That’s the exact tension I’m living in every single day. There is a massive, suffocating gap between my interior life and the "Nurse Alycia" mask I feel pressured to wear. 

Because I have those credentials, people—doctors, nurses, even my own family—seem to assume I’ve got this handled. They speak to me in medical terms, assuming I understand the jargon and the prognosis, which shuts down any space for me to just say, "I’m scared, and I don't know what I'm doing." When I’m with a provider, I feel like I’m being graded. I find myself giving these perfectly articulated summaries of her health, watching their eyes light up like, *Oh, good, she knows what’s going on,* and all the while, I’m screaming inside because I just want to be asked how *I’m* holding up.

I’ve internalized that expectation, too. I’m my own harshest critic. If I feel a wave of anxiety, my brain immediately tells me, "You know the stats on postpartum depression, you know the signs—you should be able to manage this." I feel like I’m not *allowed* to struggle because I have the tools to prevent it. It’s a performative kind of motherhood where I feel like I have to prove I’m competent before I’m allowed to be a person. It’s exhausting.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I thought my identity would be a clean, additive process—that I’d simply fold "Mother" into "Alycia the Pediatric Nurse" and become some kind of super-version of myself. I envisioned myself balancing it perfectly, the professional expertise only making me a more efficient, calm parent. I expected to feel like a woman who had "leveled up."

The reality has been more like an identity collision. The "Nurse Alycia" part of me—that cold, analytical, diagnostic machine—is currently cannibalizing the "New Mom Alycia" who just wants to bond with her daughter. I look in the mirror and I don't see the woman who had a career and a plan; I see a twenty-one-year-old girl who is terrified of the dark. 

I’ve realized that my identity wasn't just added to; it’s been stripped down. All the status and the scrubs and the confidence I carried at work? They didn't cross the threshold of my apartment. I’m finding that the professional side of me is actually a defense mechanism, a wall I build so I don’t have to confront how small and scared I really feel. I’m grieving the version of myself that didn't know these anxieties, and I’m honestly still terrified that if I let the "Nurse" mask drop entirely, there won’t be anyone left to take care of the baby.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those appointments to be the moments where I’d finally be allowed to exhale. Since I spend my life in hospitals, I viewed the doctor’s office as a sanctuary of logic—a place where if I brought my concerns, they’d be met with clear, objective solutions. I expected them to be a partner in this transition, someone who would look past my chart and see that I was struggling with the emotional weight of it all.

In reality, the appointments felt like being on the other side of a glass wall. Because I have that medical background, the visits were incredibly transactional. The providers would see my credentials and skip the basic education, jumping straight into technical updates or clinical shorthand. They checked my physical recovery—which was fine—but they never really checked *me*. They’d look at my daughter, check her growth charts, and then look at me with this "you’ve got this" nod.

It felt like they were validating my nursing brain but completely ignoring the terrified twenty-one-year-old underneath it. I’d walk out of those clinics feeling more isolated than when I walked in. I needed them to see me not as a colleague or a "low-risk patient," but as a new mother who needed permission to be vulnerable. Instead, I just left feeling like I had to keep up the charade until the very end.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven't gone back to the floor yet—I'm still in that weird, suspended state of postpartum leave—but the thought of walking back through those hospital doors is already paralyzing me. 

Before this, I thought it would be a relief. I imagined I’d miss the structure, the clear objectives, and the feeling of being "useful." I thought I’d be able to compartmentalize: step into my scrubs, be the nurse, and then go home to be the mom. I told myself it would provide a sense of normalcy, a return to the person who had control.

Now, the reality feels more like a threat. I’m terrified that if I walk back into the NICU, the triggers will be everywhere. How am I supposed to coach another woman through her baby’s distress when I’m still internally shaking from my own 3 a.m. panic? I’m scared that the moment I see a patient, I’ll lose my professional mask and start sobbing, or worse, that I’ll bring the clinical cynicism home with me and start treating my own daughter like a case study instead of my child. 

I’m terrified that I’ll have to choose between being a good nurse and being a present mother. It feels like I’m being forced to abandon one part of myself to save the other, and I don't know how to exist in the space in between.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

I’m still staring down the barrel of returning to work, and the "practical" side of it is already making me feel like I’m suffocating. I thought I’d have the feeding schedule mapped out, the childcare locked in—I’m a nurse, after all, I’m supposed to be the master of logistics. But the reality is that the logistics feel like they’re being built on quicksand.

Thinking about pumping in the back room of the unit, while I'm surrounded by the exact monitors and alarms that keep my brain in a state of high alert? It feels like it’s going to be impossible to disconnect. I’m already spiraling about the logistics—how do I keep my supply up when the shift work is so brutal? What happens if I’m called into a code when I’m supposed to be expressing milk? 

And then there's the childcare. Leaving her with anyone else, even someone qualified, feels like a betrayal of that hyper-vigilance I’ve developed. I know the stats on infant safety in daycare, I know the risks of sleep environments, and I’m terrified that I’m going to be sitting at the nursing station, not focusing on my patients, but frantically checking my phone for updates. I’m realizing that my "knowledge" is actually a liability here—it's just fuel for the anxiety fire. I’m not just worried about the work; I’m worried that I’m going to lose my mind trying to reconcile my two worlds.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I think I went in with a very idealized, "team-based" vision. We talked about it, of course—how we’d split the night feedings, how we’d rotate the chores. I honestly expected that we’d be a unit, standing side-by-side in this. Because I’m a nurse, I think I expected us to operate like a surgical team: efficient, clear roles, no wasted motion.

But the reality is much more fragmented. It’s hard to have a "partnership" when I’m the one physically recovering and, more importantly, when I’m the one who can’t turn off that clinical alarm in my head. There are nights where he’s trying to help, but I find myself hovering, correcting how he holds her or how he’s checking her diaper, because my "nurse brain" is convinced his way isn't "safe" enough. It’s not fair to him, and it makes me feel like a controlling, miserable person. 

We’re both exhausted, and the silence in the apartment often feels like a barrier between us. He sees me as "the mom" who knows everything, so he leans on me for the decisions, but he doesn't see the fear behind those decisions. I’m carrying so much of the mental load—the monitoring, the researching, the worrying—that I feel more like a single mother who just happens to share a bedroom with a roommate. It’s isolating. I want to be his partner again, but right now, I’m just his anxious, tired shadow.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise? It’s the silence—or more accurately, the unspoken expectations we’ve both fallen into. 

I think I’m surprised by how easily I’ve slipped into this "gatekeeper" role. I honestly didn't think I would be that person. Before, I was all about teamwork and collaboration. But once the baby was home, a switch flipped. I find myself watching him from across the room, and if he does something that contradicts the "best practice" way I’ve internalized, I jump in. It’s like I’m policing him, which is the last thing I want for us. 

And for him, it’s like he’s deferred to me entirely because he thinks I’m the "expert." He sees me check her oxygen levels or fret over her sleep environment and assumes that’s just "Nurse Alycia" being competent. He doesn't realize that I’m doing it because I’m drowning in fear. He’s taking a step back, thinking he’s giving me space, but it just feels like he’s leaving me alone in the trenches. 

We used to talk about everything, but now we’re just two tired people moving in circles around a bassinet. I’m surprised by how lonely you can feel even when there’s someone else right there in the room with you.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be the ultimate safety net. I thought that by following the guidelines, attending the check-ups, and being a professional in the field, the system would essentially hold me. I thought it would be a series of checkpoints where my physical and mental health were monitored just as closely as the baby's. I walked in expecting a partnership, believing that because I was "medium risk" and a nurse, I would be understood, validated, and kept safe.

The reality, looking back, is that I was treated like a participant in a clinical trial rather than a woman becoming a mother. The system didn't want my fears; it wanted my clinical cooperation. The most significant gap was the absence of the *human* element. I received incredible technical care—the medicine worked, the protocols were followed, the baby is here and she is safe—but I felt utterly invisible. 

No one ever checked on the woman behind the charts. They saw the nurse, they saw the patient, but they didn't see the twenty-one-year-old girl who was terrified of her own shadow. I needed someone to break the silence and ask me, "How are *you* doing?" instead of "Do you have any clinical questions?" I’m left feeling like I survived the procedure, but I'm still trying to survive the transition.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that still sits heavy in my chest is the idea of "permission." 

I spent my whole life training for a world where "I don't know" is a dangerous sentence. In the unit, a guess can cost a life. But here, in this quiet apartment, "I don't know" is just the truth. I think I wanted—and still need—someone to tell me that it’s okay for the nurse to step aside and for the mom to be confused. 

I’m struggling with this feeling that I’ve "failed" because I’m experiencing the typical anxiety of a first-time mother, even though I know the pathophysiology behind it. I feel like I’m cheating if I admit I’m overwhelmed because I have the educational privilege to know better. 

I just want to say that being a parent isn't a medical procedure you can study for. It’s a messy, terrifying, beautiful human experience. I think the system needs to realize that even the people who *give* the care—nurses, doctors, technicians—need to be *cared for*. We aren't just conduits for information. We are people who get scared at 3 a.m. too. I just wish one person had looked at me and reminded me that I’m allowed to be human, even if I’m a nurse. That would have changed everything.
